title: AlUla Part 2: A Graveyard of Empires
_note: PART 2 — deliberately a FRESH angle vs the user's Part 1 (which already covered Thamud, the she-camel, the punishment, and the 'enter weeping' hadith). This one is about AlUla as a stack of MULTIPLE lost civilizations — a 'did you know' that barely overlaps Part 1. NO RECITAL / no ayah audio by request: the Qur'an is referenced only as on-screen TEXT captions, never recited — so there is also ZERO copyright exposure. TTS pronunciation: AlUla='al-OO-la', al-Hijr='al-HIJR', Thamud='tha-MOOD', Dadan='da-DAN', Lihyan='LIH-yan', Nabataeans='nab-a-TEE-ans'. ACCURACY: presents the Qur'anic account (Thamud at al-Hijr) and the archaeological record (Dadan, Lihyan, Nabataeans) side by side without forcing a disputed chronology between them; the Hegra tombs are credited to the Nabataeans. For #HistoryTikTokContest (1min+).

segments.text: Everyone films AlUla's tombs. Almost nobody tells you the real secret of this valley in north-west Saudi Arabia. It isn't one lost city. It's layer after layer of vanished kingdoms, stacked on the same few miles of desert.
segments.caption: AlUla · north-west Arabia

segments.text: Start with the one the Quran names. This is the land of al-Hijr, the home of Thamud, a people who carved their houses into the mountains, and were destroyed for their arrogance.
segments.caption: Quran · Surah Al-Hijr 15:80-84

segments.text: But Thamud were only the beginning. The reason empire after empire fought for this valley comes down to one thing: geography. AlUla sat on the incense road, the ancient highway that carried frankincense and myrrh north out of Arabia to the whole known world.
segments.caption: The incense road

segments.text: Whoever controlled this oasis taxed that trade and grew enormously rich. So first rose the kingdom of Dadan, an oasis power already flourishing nearly three thousand years ago.
segments.caption: Kingdom of Dadan (Dedan)

segments.text: Then came the kingdom of Lihyan, one of the largest Arabian kingdoms of its age, whose giant stone statues are still being pulled out of the sand today.
segments.caption: Lihyanite kingdom · c. 5th-2nd century BCE

segments.text: And finally the Nabataeans, the same master carvers who built Petra. They cut the breathtaking tombs of Hegra, the ones every tourist photographs and assumes is the whole story.
segments.caption: Nabataeans · Hegra

segments.text: Four powers. One valley. Each one certain it would last forever. Every single one of them is now silent stone.
segments.caption: Dadan · Lihyan · Nabataeans · and Thamud

segments.text: And this is exactly what the Quran tells us to go and notice. Travel through the land, it says, and see how the story of the people before you actually ended.
segments.caption: Quran · Surah Ar-Rum 30:42

segments.text: So AlUla was never just a luxury backdrop. It is a graveyard of empires that each believed they were untouchable, left standing as a sign for anyone willing to actually look.
segments.caption: A sign for those who reflect.

sources: Qur'anic account: Thamud in the land of al-Hijr, carving homes in the mountains and destroyed for arrogance — Surah Al-Hijr 15:80-84 (also 7:73-79; 26:149). Referenced as on-screen text only; NOT recited.
Dadan (Dedan): ancient oasis kingdom centred at al-Khuraybah, AlUla; a major caravan-trade power in the early-to-mid 1st millennium BCE (also named as a trading people in the Hebrew Bible).
Lihyan (Lihyanite kingdom): succeeded Dadan, c. 5th-2nd century BCE, one of the largest Arabian kingdoms; known for monumental Lihyanite statues excavated at Dadan.
Nabataeans: 1st c. BCE - 1st c. CE; built Hegra (Mada'in Salih) as their southern city (Petra was the capital). The famous rock-cut tombs at AlUla are Nabataean.
Incense road: AlUla/Dadan was a key station on the caravan route carrying frankincense and myrrh from southern Arabia northward to the Mediterranean world.
Lesson framing: 'Travel through the land and observe how was the end of those before' — Surah Ar-Rum 30:42 (cf. 27:69). On-screen text only.

ACCURACY NOTE: the script presents the Qur'anic (Thamud) and archaeological (Dadan/Lihyan/Nabataean) records side by side and does NOT assert a precise chronological order between Qur'anic Thamud and the dated kingdoms, since the dating of Qur'anic Thamud is debated. Hegra's tombs are credited to the Nabataeans, not Thamud.
